did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used an improved MobileNet convolutional neural network (CNN) for the auxiliary diagnosis of actinic keratosis (AK) versus non-actinic keratoses (NAK). The model was trained and tested on the HAM10000 dataset and validated on an external ISIC dataset, achieving an accuracy of 0.9265 and an AUC of 0.97.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and validation of an improved MobileNet model for actinic keratosis diagnosis. It details specific methods (data preprocessing, network architecture modification), datasets used (HAM10000 and external ISIC dataset), and reports new quantitative results (accuracy, AUC, training time) from five-fold cross-validation and external testing, comparing its performance against other deep learning models.

title: An Actinic Keratosis Auxiliary Diagnosis Method Based on an Enhanced MobileNet Model
brief_summary: This study developed an enhanced MobileNet convolutional neural network for the auxiliary diagnosis of actinic keratosis (AK) versus non-actinic keratoses (NAK). The model was trained and tested on the HAM10000 dataset using custom preprocessing involving Lab color space and contrast-limited adaptive histogram equalization. Performance was validated using an external independent dataset from the ISIC 2019 challenge to assess generalization capability. The improved MobileNet achieved an accuracy of 0.9265 and an AUC of 0.97, outperforming baseline MobileNet, ResNet, GoogleNet, EfficientNet, and Xception. Additionally, the model demonstrated superior efficiency with the shortest training time of 821.7 seconds among the compared models.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for the binary classification task. The study focuses on diagnosing AK using 327 skin images from the HAM10000 dataset and 867 images from the external ISIC 2019 dataset.

The negative class in the binary classification task. Includes conditions such as seborrheic keratosis and geriatric skin keratosis. The study used 1099 skin images diagnosed as NAK from the HAM10000 dataset.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of Actinic Keratosis (AK) versus Non-Actinic Keratoses (NAK)
all_task_types.details: The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (AK vs. NAK) using an enhanced MobileNet convolutional neural network. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, MCC, and AUC on the HAM10000 dataset (via five-fold cross-validation) and an external ISIC 2019 dataset.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: The HAM10000 dataset, collected by the Department of Dermatology of the University of Vienna, Austria, and the Cliff Rosendahl Skin Cancer Practice in Queensland, Australia. It contains 100,015 images of seven common skin diseases. For this study, a subset was used including 327 images diagnosed as actinic keratosis (AK) and 1099 images diagnosed as non-actinic keratoses (NAK).

use.training: Used for training and testing via five-fold cross-validation after data preprocessing (Lab color space, CLAHE, and augmentation).
use.validation: Used for validation via five-fold cross-validation to improve stability and generalization.
use.testing: Used for testing via five-fold cross-validation.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: An external independent dataset released by The International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) in 2019. From the original 1011 AK images and 2624 Benign Keratosis-like Lesion (BKL) images, 867 AK images and 540 BKL images were available for use after removing duplicates with the HAM10000 dataset.

use.validation: Used for external independent testing to validate the preprocessing approach and assess the model's generalization capability on unseen data.
use.other: Used to verify the broad applicability of the preprocessing method and robustness of the model.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To improve the stability and generalization of the model and to evaluate performance metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, MCC) on the HAM10000 dataset.

To report the uncertainty and variability of performance metrics (Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, MCC) across the five-fold cross-validation folds for the improved MobileNet and comparative models.
To validate the preprocessing approach and assess the model's generalization capability and robustness on unseen data from the ISIC 2019 dataset.
To compare the performance and efficiency of the improved MobileNet against baseline models (Unmodified MobileNet, ResNet, GoogleNet, EfficientNet, Xception).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). Performance comparisons were made using mean values with 95% confidence intervals (CI) and absolute metric values (accuracy, AUC, training time) without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.notes: The Modified MobileNet achieved the highest accuracy and AUC among all compared models. It also had the shortest training time. The total time for five-fold cross-validation on local devices was 821.7 seconds.

ISIC 2019 Challenge Dataset.notes: The Modified MobileNet convincingly outperformed other mainstream models on the external dataset, demonstrating robustness and adaptability to unseen data.

architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported for the Enhanced MobileNet; comparative models (ResNet50, InceptionV3, EfficientNet) used pre-trained weights.
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported for the Enhanced MobileNet; ResNet50 input size was 224x224.

modifications: Custom preprocessing: Lab color space conversion and Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE)
Retained only the first 15 layers of the original MobileNet

learning_rate.reduction_strategy: ReduceLROnPlateau callback; halved when validation accuracy did not improve for 2 consecutive epochs; reduced down to 0.00001

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully connected layer with 2 output units

preprocessing_techniques: Color space conversion from RGB to Lab color space to separate brightness from color and extend the color gamut
Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) applied to the brightness channel to enhance local contrast and highlight textured areas
Data augmentation using TensorFlow ImageDataGenerator including random rotation, translation, scaling, horizontal flipping, and vertical flipping

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied using TensorFlow ImageDataGenerator to generate novel image variants and prevent overfitting.

conclusions: The proposed enhanced MobileNet model achieved high accuracy (0.9265) and AUC (0.97) for the auxiliary diagnosis of actinic keratosis (AK) versus non-actinic keratoses (NAK), outperforming baseline MobileNet, ResNet, GoogleNet, EfficientNet, and Xception.
The improved MobileNet demonstrated superior computational efficiency with the shortest training time (821.7 seconds) among the compared models, making it suitable for clinical deployment.

External independent testing on the ISIC 2019 dataset confirmed the model's robustness, generalization capability, and the broad applicability of the custom preprocessing method (Lab color space and CLAHE) on unseen data.

The custom preprocessing techniques (Lab color space conversion and Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization) effectively enhanced image contrast and highlighted lesion features, improving model identification capabilities.
The method has significant clinical translational value and potential for embedding into portable medical devices to assist physicians in efficient and accurate AK diagnosis.
important_remarks: The study focuses exclusively on a binary classification task (Actinic Keratosis vs. Non-Actinic Keratoses), limiting its generalizability to other skin cancer types or multi-class dermatological diagnoses.

The external validation dataset (ISIC 2019) was significantly smaller than the training dataset, comprising only 1,407 images (867 AK and 540 BKL) after removing duplicates, which may limit the robustness of the generalization assessment.

The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) to confirm the statistical significance of performance differences between the proposed model and comparators, relying instead on mean values with 95% confidence intervals.

The model was trained and tested on a relatively small subset of the HAM10000 dataset (1,426 images total), which may increase the risk of overfitting despite the use of data augmentation and five-fold cross-validation.
The study acknowledges that the current dataset coverage is limited and calls for expanding the dataset to improve model performance and clinical applicability.
The research is positioned as filling a gap in specific research for actinic keratosis identification, noting that previous deep learning research in this specific area was scarce.
The authors declare no conflicts of interest, and the study was funded by a specific hospital talent introduction fund, which should be considered when assessing potential bias.
